replies.title: Tree/Body
replies.content:
My understanding is that the Tree of Life is somehow related to the centers of the body, and used for developing various spiritual levels. Is this right?

And if so, would that subsequently mean that the Tree was based on the form of the physical body? And as well based in a way upon the structure of the macrocosm?

(My current belief is that human “centers” are not innate to us, but must be intentionally formed. Therefore, there is some flexibility in their placement and make-up…)

replies.title: Re: Tree/Body
replies.content:
1) The Tree of Life can be related to centers in the body, if you choose to relate it. There are, inevitably, several different ways of doing that.

2) The Tree was originally derived from geometry. (It's a product of Greek Neopythagorean philosophy, picked up by the Gnostics in the first century and absorbed into Judaism in the eleventh century.) Later on, it was correlated to both microcosm and macrocosm -- again, in various ways.

3) I've always appreciated the claim that there are 360 energy centers in the body, and each spiritual tradition chooses some small subset of those to activate.

replies.title: Re: Tree/Body
replies.content:
So, 360 energy centers? Well, OK, that's fine with me, but how do those "energy centers" get defined?

My chiropractor uses applied kinesiology, which maps out a corresponding circulatory rebalancing point as well as a lymph-drainage rebalancing point for every single muscle in the body. He also works with the TCM meridians with their vast network of chi nodes that can need adjustment. Then there's the various systems of polarity points that need to get worked in tandem in order to rebalance. Add to that the various schools of chiropractic that focus on one particular governing bone or joint, such as the atlas or the sacrum, whose proper adjustment then resets the entire body. Throw in all the reflexology points on the extremeties and we clearly have thousands of adjustable energy centers or at least reference points in our bodies. And that's only taking into account one practitioner's limited training and experience.

There's also complex maps of chakras running throughout the body, which ayurvedic medicine uses. I've seen some people map out the column of major chakras that runs up the center of our bodies to include hundreds of major chakras extending above our heads and below our feet, though it would be a stretch to claim that those could be defined as energy centers "in the body". Anyway, the folks that focus on 360 of the body's energy centers, what types of energy centers are they talking about? Do they come at it from one particular discipline, and what practices do they find work with those centers to effect what changes?

Meanwhile, God bless the tree of life for providing us with a concise little subdivision of ten or eleven energetic foci to practice with. Babylonians, with their base-sixty system and times tables 3600 figures long, could probably conceptualize in ways we can't even begin to imagine, but I am truly grateful for our own simplified little base-ten cipher!

— Christophe

replies.title: Re: Tree/Body
replies.content:
The 360 energy centers, as I was taught, include every organ in the astral and etheric bodies, doing every kind of energy process that takes place at every level of each body. There are 33 of them along the spine, one for each vertebra. This is why sources disagree about where the 2nd and 3rd chakras are -- different systems use different points for these two.

There are 9 more centers in the midline of the skull rising up in a line from the base of the skull to the crown chakra -- those 33 + 9 centers along the midline are supposed to be the 42 assessor-spirits in the Egyptian Book of the Dead and the 42 letters in one of the Jewish names of God. The other 318 centers are elsewhere in the body. Each center can be activated in various ways and can have various effects, which is why every spiritual system chooses a handful of centers and works with them in specific ways to get the effects that further that specific path.

comments.title: Hello!
comments.content:
First of all, I love the way your mind works. Based on what I've read, I think you're some combination of philosopher and wizard. And I am here for it.

To start, I was wondering what you think about chaos magic. I'm also curious about what you think about William S Burroughs and the cut-up method. Is it a legit form of divination?

replies.title: Re: Hello!
replies.content:
1) Well, "philosopher" and "wizard" are both labels that Greek writers used for the Druids, so I must be doing something right. ;-)

2) I'm not really a fan of chaos magic. I read Liber Null and Psychonaut when it first came out, and I also read the Illuminatus! trilogy -- the not-always-acknowledged foundational text for a lot of chaos magic -- when that first came out; I found it all rather bland. I find a cosmos full of conscious beings independent of my will, some of them far greater than human, much more interesting than a blank slate peopled only by such symbolic constructions as I create. I also find traditional occult methods more effective than the much narrower toolkit that chaos magic uses. That said, of course, your mileage may vary.

3) Any form of divination is legitimate if it produces accurate predictions. I haven't used the cut-up technique as a divinatory method, or for that matter a literary one -- my approach to writing is rather more old-fashioned! If you get accurate results with it, though, then by all means. I do want to stress that little word "accurate;" a method of divination that fails to yield correct predictions is a waste of time.

replies.title: Re: Hello!
replies.content:
It is wild to get a response from you! Thank you.

The only thing you're not doing right is not having a podcast. At least, as far as I know.

As for chaos magic, Gordon White does it, and his perspective seems not too far off from yours. I wonder what the best way to choose a magical practice is? I suspect I'm attracted to chaos magic because it seems like there's no barrier to entry. You can just do the thing. Write the desire. Make a sigil. Boom. It's like the law of attraction (but edgier).

As for cut-ups, I'll give them a proper go and get back to you. I've only tried them for a Burroughs related project.

Anyway, thank you again for responding. What a thrill!


--Rebecca

replies.title: Re: Hello!
replies.content:
1) Thank you. I don't have the time or the technical skill to do a podcast; I make up for it by appearing on pretty much any podcast that's interested in having me.

2) If it appeals to you, by all means. As for choosing a practice, I recommend dabbling in several things, doing a lot of reading (or the online equivalent), and seeing what speaks to you. It really is a personal thing.

replies.title: Re: Hello!
replies.content:
Hey there...

I like Burroughs to a degree, but his friend Brion Gysin I appreciate even more for his output as a visual artist and as creator of the dream machine. It does seem that the future can leak out when using the cut up method as Burroughs wrote about:

"“Now these experiments started not on tape-recorders but on paper. In 1959 Brion Gysin said that writing is fifty years behind painting and applied the montage technique to words on a page and this technique had already been used at that time in painting for fifty years, it was, in fact, kind of old hat in painting. Brion copied out phrases from newspapers and magazines, then took his scissors and cut these selections into pieces and rearranged the fragments at random and these cut-up experiments appeared in Minutes To Go in 1959. When you experiment with cut-ups over a period of time, you find that some of the cut-ups and rearranged texts seem to refer to future events. I cut up an article on.. written by John Paul Getty and got “it’s a bad thing to sue your own father”, (this was a rearrangement and wasn’t in the original text), and a year later one of his sons did sue him. We had no explanation for this at the time, (perhaps, suggesting that when you cut into the present the future leaks out), but we simply accepted it and continued the experiments…" -Burroghs

These experiences, and Gysin's experience of being cursed while in Morroco did leave the two of them to explore aspects of the occult. Their writings on The Third Mind I think are at least worth looking into if you are already drawn to them and their material...

As you may know, the term "Heavy Metal" was first used by Burroughs in his cut-up novel The Soft Machine, and came from the character Uranian Willie The Heavy Metal Kid... It's interesting how that term got picked up and used as a term for the very popular genre of music.

Stay heavy,

JPM

comments.title: Ecosophia Prayer List
comments.content:
At this page is the full list of all of the requests for prayer that have recently appeared at ecosophia.net and ecosophia.dreamwidth.org, as well as in the comments of the prayer list posts (printable version here, 7/14). Please feel free to add any or all of the requests to your own prayers.

If I missed anybody, or if you would like to add a prayer request for yourself or anyone who has given you consent (or for whom a relevant person holds power of consent) to the list, please feel free to leave a comment below.

* * *

This week I would like to bring special attention to the following prayer requests, selected from the fuller list.


May Marko's newborn son Noah be blessed with good health, and may his partner Viktoria swiftly make a full recovery from childbirth and c-section.

May Brother Kornhoer's son Travis's fistula heal, may his body have the strength to fight off infections, may his kidneys strengthen, and may his empty nose syndrome abate, so that he may have a full and healthy life ahead of him.

May Princess Cutekitten, who is sick of being sick, be healed of her ailments.

May Jack H.'s father John continue to heal from his ailments, including alcohol dependency and breathing difficulties, as much as Providence allows, to be able to enjoy more time together with his loved ones.

May she be safely healed of the physical condition that has provoked her emotions; and may she be healed of the spiritual condition that brings her to be so unsettled by it. May she come to feel calm and secure in her physical body, regardless of its level of health.

May Pierre and Julie conceive a healthy baby together. May the conception, pregnancy, birth, and recovery all be healthy and smooth for baby and for Julie.

May SLClaire's honorary daughter Beth, who is undergoing dialysis for kidney disease, be blessed, and may her kidneys be restored to full functioning.

May 1Wanderer's partner Cathy, who has bravely fought against cancer to the stage of remission, now be relieved of the unpleasant and painful side-effects from the follow-up hormonal treatment, together with the stress that this imposes on both parties; may she quickly be able to resume a normal life, and the cancer not return.

May Kallianeira's partner Patrick, who passed away on May 7th, be blessed and aided in his soul's onward journey. And may Kallianeira be soothed and strengthened to successfully cope in the face of this sudden loss.

(Healing work is also welcome. Note: Healing Hands should be fine, but if offering energy work which could potentially conflict with another, please first leave a note in comments or write to randomactsofkarmasc to double check that it's safe)

May David Spangler (the esoteric teacher), who has been responding well to chemotherapy for his bladder cancer, be blessed, healed, and filled with positive energy such that he makes a full recovery.

May Giulia (Julia) in the Eastern suburbs of Cleveland Ohio be quickly healed of recurring seizures and paralysis of her left side and other neurological problems associated with a cyst on the right side of her brain and with surgery and drugs to treat it, if providence would have it, and if not, may her soul move on from this world and find peace with a minimum of further suffering for her and her family and friends.

May Debra Roberts, who has just been diagnosed with Stage 4 lung cancer, be blessed and healed to the extent that providence allows. Healing work is also welcome.

May Jack H's father John, whose aortic dissection is considered inoperable and likely fatal by his current doctors, be healed, and make a physical recovery to the full extent that providence allows, and be able to enjoy more time together with his loved ones.

May Frank R. Hartman, who lost his house in the Altadena fire, and all who have been affected by the larger conflagration be blessed and healed.

May Open Space's friend's mother
Judith be blessed and healed for a complete recovery from cancer.



* * *

Guidelines for how long prayer requests stay on the list, how to word requests, how to be added to the weekly email list, how to improve the chances of your prayer being answered, and several other common questions and issues, are to be found at the Ecosophia Prayer List FAQ.

If there are any among you who might wish to join me in a bit of astrological timing, I pray each week for the health of all those with health problems on the list on the astrological hour of the Sun on Sundays, bearing in mind the Sun's rulerships of heart, brain, and vital energies. A speculative one (but not a new one, I imagine) : I was thinking about the relationship between karma and shadow projection. I can see that when triggered by seemingly innocuous behavior of another person, it could be past deeds/attitudes that bleed into the present to teach a lesson (there's worse ways to receive lessons, granted). Are there other possible mecanisms to account for this dynamic ? Non-karmic sources to shadow projection ?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The heart of shadow projection is that you see in others what you refuse to see in yourself. Not all your current choices are a function of karma! So if there's something about yourself that's a matter of present choices but it's not something about which you're willing to be honest with yourself, it gets projected. ]

Consider a person who ends up having the hots for his neighbor's wife. There's nothing karmic about it -- he just finds her attractive, and instead of being honest with himself and saying "Sure, she's hot, but she's married and so am I so no dice," he pretends to himself that he's not attracted to her at all. (We'll say it's because of religious qualms -- that's common enough.) What will he do? Well, he may project his desires on her, and convince himself that she's making eyes at him but he's too virtuous to act on it; or he may project his desires onto his neighbor, and end up convincing himself that his neighbor is after his wife; or he may project them onto his own wife, and suspect her of adultery; or he may do more than one of these. Karma doesn't make any of this happen -- though such things generate a lot of toxic karma!

2) If somebody is trying to die, it's needless cruelty to force them to keep living. Of course it's best to do everything possible to treat someone who has a chance of recovery, but if that's not going to happen, it's far more merciful to let a natural death happen than to keep the soul trapped in an artificially animated corpse.

3) Those are perfectly good ways of benefiting the soul in its transition. Prayer, in particular, is important.

4) That's an interesting speculation. I have to say, though, that young people have no shortage of other reasons to be anxious and mentally stressed!

replies.content:
I'll pitch in on your last question. I think the anxiety and generally poor mental health amongst younger folks is due to a huge number of converging and interacting causes. That having been said, one element that I am convinced is a significant contributor that is almost entirely ignored is the lack of elders within our society. This isn't my original thought, it comes by way of Simon Sheridan (https://simonsheridan.me/blog/) who writes a very interesting little blog on where he often discusses the state of society through the lens of Jung.

The cliff note version is that, from an archetypal perspective, you start as a child with a child archetype and then you proceed through to an adult archetype then to an elder archetype. For a person to undergo the transition from a child archetype to an adult one requires that they go through a personal "journey" to do so and that the child is shown the potential for that journey by an elder. It must be an elder and the elder does not take the journey with them or help them, their role is to show them that the path is there and stand back to let them navigate it on their own. This is my bad Jungian paraphrasing and you'd be better to go find the string of fascinating articles from Simon.

Anyway, the reasoning goes that the boomers never actually made the transition to adulthood. They stayed in their defining child archetype which was the innocent (note that we are talking about the entirety of society here, not every individual, this is a generalization. Then again if you're a boomer and your first thought is to scream "NO NO NO! I'M AN ELDER"... this may apply to you). The result for the following generations is that, because the boomers generally are still stuck in a child archetype, they never transitioned through the whole thing to become elders. So, as a younger person say a millennial or zoomer, you also end up stuck in a child archetype because there are no elders around to start your journey this leaves them also stuck in a child archetype (the orphan in the case of millennials). Frustratingly, you end up stuck in a world that is absolutely crammed to the brim with old people and not an elder in sight. The old folks tend to act, most commonly in my experience, like teenagers with the good aspects of a 17 year old showing when their life is going well and the bad aspects showing when their life starts going awry. This also helps explain the extreme self-absorption and lack of sympathy that tends to be found amongst the boomers (again, this is a generalization but, if your fist reaction is to scream, "NO NOT ME! I'M NOT SELF-ABSORBED!"....)

Those articles from Simon detailing this were a bit of an epiphany for my wife and I as they match in many ways the reality that we inhabit and many behaviors and experiences have become much more clear in light of his theory. I mean this both in applying to our parents (boomers) and to us (millennials).

I can heartily recommend a dive into Simon's blog.

HV

replies.title: Dollar General

I’m visiting my parents here in central Texas, and we’ve been driving on the highway a lot. There’s a Dollar General and Bush’s Chicken on every little bump along the road; and it occurred to me that we Americans try to interpret and define our Reality through familiar chains or symbols like these.
(There are now identical giant-sized “funky chickens”, made across the border, at each and every ranch gate…)

I’m wondering, as civilization slowly collapses, if America will transfer what it deems its highest priorities over to other — more worthwhile — cultural fixtures instead.

Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Dollar General
replies.content: Historically speaking, the opposite happens: as a civilization dies, most people in it give the highest priorities to things that aren't worthwhile at all...and that's an important part of why a civilization dies.  The preservation of the best -- well, that depends on what you personally think is the best, doesn't it? What will survive, will survive because individuals -- never groups or communities or other collectivities, but individuals, usually acting in the face of mass opposition -- fling themselves into the task of making it survive.

This brought up one of my favorite Gurdjieff quotes. He’s speaking of the nature of knowledge:

"There are periods in the life of humanity, which generally coincide with the beginning of the fall of cultures and civilizations, when the masses irretrievably lose their reason and begin to destroy everything that has been created by centuries and millenniums of culture.

In Search of the Miraculous, chapter 2

comments.title: Reality Reflecting Stories
comments.content:
Good Morning JMG,

I have been meditating lately on a topic brought up a couple weeks ago, that in creating a work of fiction we run the risk of manifesting elements of that fiction into reality. It reminded me of an anecdote I read about Stephen King, who wrote a character who was wheelchair bound for his Dark Tower series and then found himself in a wheelchair for a while.

Now, I write comics, which take a lot more work per story beat than novels - an exchange between characters that takes a minute to write can easily take 8 hours or more to draw. It seems logical to me that any effect one might get from concentrating while writing a scene would have a stronger effect from doing comics. I doubt this effect would apply as much to corporate-led team efforts like superhero comics, although I note the history of superhero comic publishers often resembles the same nonsensical unending tragicomic farce with big personalities that they put in their products. In solo comic productions, I would say there is a very strong correlation between the creator and the products, at least as strong a resemblance as that between people and their pets. I don't know how you would separate the influence of the work on the author from that of the author on the work.

If there was a prophetic type of influence, wouldn't it be just as likely to be glimpsing nonlinear time as to be manifesting the event through intention?

Since my comics are generally fantastical, lighthearted and warm, I wouldn't be concerned with any manifesting or prophetic influence. However, I have written some tragedy into the story as well, because a story about a bunch of people who like each other and where nothing goes wrong is not very interesting.

In my comic, a family that is slightly similar to mine has lost a child from diabetes that was untreatable due to postindustrial collapse, an element I added with the advice of the Ecosophia commentariat. It is so far only a backstory element and the parts where it will get to be addressed more directly instead of light allusions have yet to be fully composed. However, since adding that in, we have had a child develop pre-diabetes and last week we had a sudden miscarriage (prayers welcome).

I would hate to think that my quest to produce a good story was having deleterious effects on my family's health. If it is, any advice on avoiding negative real-world effects while still writing good stories would be welcome.

replies.title: Re: Reality Reflecting Stories
replies.content:
Hmm. I don't think that tragedies in novels cause tragedies in real life, because the crucial factor of intention is absent. On the other hand, it sometimes happens that writers and other storytellers pick up on events before they happen -- very often in exaggerated form. In Stephen King's case, for example, if I recall correctly the character was permanently in a wheelchair, but King was not -- he may have perceived intuitively that he'd spend some time in a wheelchair in his future, and that found its way into his story.

There's also a personal element. None of the tragedies in my life have appeared in advance in my fiction, and none of the tragedies in my fiction -- and there have been quite a few! -- have appeared in my life.

I guess one would take two disparate systems and try to syncretize them—find a through-line, in terms of imagery, and see what, if anything, surfaces.

I'm thinking this is, in a sense, perhaps what happened to some degree with Renaissance magic, where lingering images from antiquity managed to find through lanes into the systems of the day and find expression. You said earlier that I might be breaking new ground with looking at interactions between endurance exercise and occult practices. I have reviewed my notes and found three things that are worth sharing thus far. For context, I am going for runs 4-5 times a week, with a maximum distance of about 5 miles; and my spiritual path is that of the Hermetic Golden Dawn.

a) There is a definite link between the subtle energies of the solar plexus and the notorious side stitch, the sharp pain right below the ribs that sometimes happens while running. If I work on my solar plexus centre too soon before a run, I will reliably get a side stitch; the reverse has also happened a couple times, but seems much less common. I'm not sure if this is because it is actually harder to induce a side stitch that way, or the fact at the very least there will be a shower breaking up a run with anything after it, but I have no interest in running the necessary experiments to determine the answer.

This is also the second time I've taken up running; and the first time I got a lot of side stitches. This time, while my legs were very unhappy to start, I almost never got one. I think it is worth considering the possibility that the spiritual exercises I did to strengthen my solar plexus in the years between when I took up running the first time and when I started the second time might be why there is such a large difference between these two experiences of learning to run.

b) There are some definite, but subtle, consequences of running on the subtle bodies. The big one I've noticed is that my stronger legs are better able to handle the circulation of energy done during the middle pillar (in the form described in Circles of Power); the effect is subtle enough that I did not notice it at the time, but reviewing my notes I found that the circulation of energy improved significantly at the same time I was feeling like I was getting back into shape again.

c) I've noticed that the planet ruling the day seems to provide a subtle boost for some things related to the planet. The two most useful for running seem to be Mars and Saturn. Mars likes when people go all out, and so I've noticed a subtle but significant improvement since I shifted towards doing my most intense speed training on Tuesdays. Saturn meanwhile makes it slightly easier to both run slower (and thus avoid burning out), and towards endurance itself; and so Saturday works slightly better for the long run than any other day.

The effects on any individual run of aligning my training schedules with the planets is small, but over the course of a couple months it has added up to a noticeable improvement in my training.

comments.title: Sublimation and Degradation
comments.content:
I've been meditating through the Cos Doc and I'm still a bit unclear on what she says about sublimating a force up the planes by separating it into two components, and replacing that force by degrading a pair of forces into a single one. I get that broad concept just fine, but it's difficult for me to apply it without some concrete examples. I've tried coming up with examples but I'm not very satisfied with them.

1) Could you provide a few concrete examples of which two forces are degraded into one, and which one thing is sublimated into two, so that I might see how these things move and fill the gap left behind?

2) are the two forces that are bound and brought down always unpleasant things, or can they be positive? For example, I know that one can take two unsavory aspects of their personality and bind them to reduce their power. But if we are calling cosmic forces down into manifestation, it seems like to bless the land with particular circumstances that even good things might be combined, lessening their power but allowed to become particular. For example, could creative flow and stability be combined and brought down to get a garden to flourish?

replies.title: Re: Sublimation and Degradation
replies.content:
1) Sure. Consider an addiction. Inevitably those are driven by two strong patterns of emotional force in conflict, of which one is admitted and one is not. An alcoholic, for example, might be caught between shame, self-loathing, and self-pity, which are his conscious emotions, and a secret rage at the people he's punishing through his addiction and its consequences. (Please note that this is only one of countless possible patterns; I'm in no way saying that this is what drives all alcoholics.) As long as those two are locked in conflict, the addiction stays; the struggle to fight the addiction (never, please note, to defeat it) helps hold it in place. That's the genius of the 12-step method -- by admitting one's powerlessness and handing the whole issue over to a higher power, tolerably often one side of the conflict is weakened and the whole thing starts to unravel.

2) If you bind two unpleasant forces, I guarantee you that the outcome will be unpleasant; the tiger doesn't change his stripes when he mates with another tiger! Of course good things can be combined. Consider the fusion of the solar and telluric currents -- that's a very good example of the positive dimension of the process.

replies.title: Re: Sublimation and Degradation
replies.content:
1) That's a helpful start. So self-loathing and rage might be the two dragons locked up here. By separating them and sublimating them, the alcoholic raises those emotions, or his consciousness of them, to a higher plane? And what two forces does he bind to bring down in their place? Fortune said it was important to get that part, otherwise the void might be filledby something you don't wan there.

2) Ah, good to know. I was thinking of pitting two opposite bad habits against one another so they sort of block each other from doing what they do, but I can see why that's not as good as dealing with each separately, sublimating them, and bringing something good (new habits) in their place.

replies.title: Re: Sublimation and Degradation
replies.content: Very often, once you release the two forces onto their natural plane, that solves itself. Let's consider the case we're discussing. Our alcoholic realizes that he's drinking himself blotto as a passive-aggressive way of punishing people in his life. It's not something that just happened to him, it's something that he's doing. That unknots the forces; he has a divided will but it's a conscious division, and so can be resolved. He may realize that he's punishing the people around him for something that happened to him in childhood, for which they have no responsibility, and deal with the unresolved emotions; he may realize that he's punishing the people around him for something that they did in the past, and decide what to do about that; or he may realize that he's punishing the people around him because they're being completely beastly to him here and now, and in that case he can stop drinking, dump them like a bag of old clothes, and go have a life instead. In each case the forces are allowed to flow freely, instead of being knotted into a binary, and they descend into manifestation in a more healthy way. What Fortune is warning about is the kind of situation where you get an alcoholic to stop drinking without resolving the conflicts that lead to drinking -- in that case, something else is just going to take its place.

replies.title: Re: Sublimation and Degradation
replies.content:
As a side note, that's interesting about the 12-step program. I know a number of people who have been through it, and it was always odd to me that they admit helplessness when it seemed that it was a stronger will that they needed. But maybe that will isn't weak, it's just divided and locked into a battle with itself that can't be resolved by more willing.

I do notice that the program works for people who admit their own role in the problem and work hard to resolve the parts of their character that cause the issues, whereas the people I know who have failed have gone through all the motions but still refuse to take responsibility (despite saying that they do) and still heap blame on others (despite saying that they don't).

replies.title: Re: Sublimation and Degradation
replies.content:
1) Exactly. Strength of will isn't the problem -- Fortune remarks somewhere that the hopeless alcoholic, who seems to have no willpower at all, will display astonishing tenacity, focus, and cleverness when it comes to getting a drink! Division of the will is the problem. The judo move where one guy suddenly stops pushing and the other guy lands on his nose is one way to resolve that -- and that's also why it's common lore among alcoholics that you can't get out of it until you've hit rock bottom. l.e., stopped fighting.

2) Bingo. The latter group simply add another knot -- "consciously I'm pretending to go through the program, subconsciously I'm sabotaging it" -- to the original one.

replies.title: Re: Astrolabe
replies.content: An astrolabe is only useful if you want to do without a clock and determine the exact altitude of the sun or an important star as a way to check the time. Astrologers who do the work by hand normally use an ephemeris and a book of tables, which includes tables of logarithms -- a slide rule is a replacement for these latter, but I encourage you to learn the tables of logarithms first. 1) Make a list of sun gods around the world: Ra, Shamash, Apollo, Surya, Amaterasu, etc., etc. There are your representation in human myth of the Solar Logos.

2) Not a misunderstanding but a very specific and rather bitter attitude. A lot of people in ancient times realized that the cycles of the heavens were taking the world away from an age of magic and direct contact with the gods, and toward an age of hard material realities -- you know, the world we inhabit now. They didn't like it at all, and they considered the astrological factors that were driving the world that way to be hostile to humanity.

3) Ah, but think for a moment of all that an ant has to do that an oak tree never does. A tree doesn't need to move any faster than it can grow, and its senses are diffuse, general, and slow. An ant must constantly assess its environment through multiple specialized senses, and has to move and act and interact with a constantly changing world of other ants, predators, food, and many other things. It's a huge leap from the plant kingdom. Mere size doesn't matter that much!

4) They're not part of any of the swarms; they're creations of the created, like elementals, but brought into being by the Lords of Form rather than the Lords of Flame.

5) I'm not at all sure where she got that! There are quite a few things in her teachings that seem to have come boiling up out of nowhere. As for the archetypal form, that's a constant theme in occult teachings from ancient times on.

comments.title: Drawing out Energy with Water Drop Visualization
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

I've stumbled across a visualization process that helps me distill my emotional energy into a colored water drop. I use this process when problems in my life give me complex emotions which obstruct my path in the day.

I start this process by imagining a drop of water suspended in air. I then add color to the drop of water, associating the colors with the emotions that are obstructing my day. Usually these emotions are tied to a problem that I perceive in my life. I then take the colors and analyze their significance, which gives me perspective on the problem at hand. After I analyze the colors in the water droplet I imagine the water drop bursting open and the water falling into a larger, colorless pond. When I burst the water drop the emotional qualities of my problem wash over my consciousness. I usually rest for a moment to allow myself the chance to process the emotions. When my consciousness finishes processing the emotions associated with my problem, I am free to go about the rest of my day unburdened by the problem's emotional weight.

I have practiced this a couple times now, and I wonder if this practice corroborates with any practices you know.

All the best!

-MRDobner

replies.title: Re: Drawing out Energy with Water Drop Visualization
replies.content: Interesting! No, I don't know of anything quite like that. If you're willing, I'd encourage you to write up a detailed description and post it somewhere, so others can benefit from it.

comments.title: Karmic ripening
comments.content:
My understanding is that karma is bastardized and oversimplified in Western culture. Instead of being directly penalized for bad behavior, or rewarded for the good, it's more like a credit/debt you build up, and eventually pay and get paid for. Often this happens over multiple lifetimes, but can happen within the same incarnation, is that correct?

This at least answers the perennial questions of why some people seemingly get away with evil, while others face needless suffering -- it's all part of a larger cycle that everyone has to answer to eventually.

Anyway, I was wondering how to burn off bad karma and cultivate the good. Is there any means to do this faster, or are we trapped on Mr Bones' Wild Ride? A shift in thought patterns, contrition for dumb/wrong things you did, offering blessings, and contributing positively to the world... those seem like viable strategies, but am I overlooking something? By accepting culpability instead of trying to evade responsibility, that might help too. Still, maybe karma is too complex for any easy solutions? Any info would be appreciated.

replies.title: Re: Karmic ripening
replies.content:
Oh, good gods, yes -- if it's any consolation, pop culture in Asian countries messes it up just as badly. Karma is simply the rule that every action has consequences for good and ill, and these inevitably circle back to the person who did the action. It's very much cumulative, and its expression can unfold over many lives. I've discussed it here:

https://www.ecosophia.net/the-law-of-consequences/

As for burning off karma in a hurry, be careful. Unless you know just how much bad karma you have and what's going to be needed to resolve it, you could give yourself a hideous fate. What if the fastest way you could resolve a dozen lives of miserably bad karma would be to be falsely accused of murdering a child, convicted, imprisoned, and murdered with a makeshift knife by one of your fellow convicts? Or to spend the next ten years in agony, dying of bowel cancer? These and things like them are real options, you know. This is why the best thing you can do is simply to clean up your act here and now, and work on generating favorable karma by blessing, healing, and helping; leave the rest to your higher self and the gods.

replies.title: Re: Karmic ripening
replies.content:
A very succinct and thorough answer, thanks! I was concerned that my "scattershot" queries might overload you, but instead you covered it pithily. So yea, I guess it's a long road, and its best to work on this gradually. After all, I theorize that my rare, lethal condition (controlled by meds) is karmic ripening, and that's enough to deal with!

I've also theorized that those stories you hear of kids dying of leukemia could very well be the "fast lane" of burning off negative karma, and that would indeed be overwhelming.

Slow and steady wins the race, it seems.

replies.title: Re: Karmic ripening
replies.content: Exactly. Karma is nothing to mess with casually, and especially if you've already got an obvious karmic issue!

comments.title: Repel intrusive thoughts?
comments.content:
I’ve lately been experiencing intrusive thoughts that I’d gladly be rid of. The background is that for a couple years and counting I’ve experienced very high levels of stress, the kind you get from frightening or downright terrifying existential threats that keep coming and go on for a very long time. And they still continue to this moment, accompanied by personal grief.

The result is intense anger on my part, toward those entities and institutions - they’re very real - from which these threats emanate. I don’t like these thoughts nor the feelings that accompany them. I’m talking about incandescent rage accompanied by some very unpleasant ideations. I don’t want the blowback that I suspect such highly charged thoughts and emotions can bring, which can only worsen my already difficult situation. Can you suggest anything I can do to be rid of them, or to protect me from them?

My background is in basic Golden Dawn rituals such as the Middle Pillar, Hellenic style. I also have some acquaintance with the archangel Michael, due to invoking the violet flame for a couple of years.

replies.title: Re: Repel intrusive thoughts?
replies.content:
Until you decrease your stress levels, there isn't much you can do that will work. Obviously I don't know your actual situation, but I've found that very consistently, the people I know who insist that they're facing constant existential threats are caught up in a tangled mess of obsession, projection, and runaway imagination which has much more to do with their own internal issues than with the outside world -- though admittedly the mass media does a fine job of fostering that sort of thing, since miserable, frightened, angry people are easier to manipulate.

One other thing to keep in mind is that anger is almost always a secondary emotion -- we make ourselves feel angry because that distracts us from some other emotion we don't want to feel. This is usually shame, fear, or grief, and normally has nothing to do with the apparent target of the anger; it's usually either childhood issues, or some conflict in current life that the person doesn't want to confront, or a combination of these. You'll release the anger, and the angry thoughts, when you deal with the primary emotion that it's concealing.

I know this is probably not at all what you want to hear, but there it is: if you just try to get rid of the angry thoughts without dealing with the source of the stress, that's not going to accomplish anything. You have to deal with your own emotional investment in the thoughts that are generating the stress.

replies.title: Re: Repel intrusive thoughts?
replies.content:
Thanks for the thoughtful and thorough response. Perhaps I may be to some extent recapitulating the crises that have happened to me during this parlous period, and maybe some old fears as well.

But this is not just about me creating my own reality. The universe has something to say. There is much to truly dread in this time of decay, notably destitution and homelessness, and I have experienced protracted exposure to these threats - not experienced them, thank the gods, but the very real threat thereof. Also the grief I have mentioned is not small. So I already know what are the emotions that underlie my anger: fear and sorrow. The harsh experiences I complain of are real, not just in my head. Moreover they flow from common injustices that are far from imaginary.

It seems to me that I need to find a way to calm down, to relax as best I can. Also - speaking of old baggage - I will acknowledge that I have always had difficulty trusting that things will work out OK. And that is indeed from personal history.

Is there some way of being carefree even in the face of adversity? I’d sure like to learn it.

replies.title: Re: Repel intrusive thoughts?
replies.content:
Harsh experiences and common injustices can be real, and still be given more importance on a day to day basis than they deserve. It's like those online financial-news sites that have predicted fourteen out of the last three recessions -- recessions are real, but constantly freaking out about the supposed imminence of one and assuming the worst are not productive habits. The same rule applies more generally.

The people I know who are carefree in the face of adversity are those who have been through adversity and come out the other side. Poverty, for example, doesn't worry me in the least; I've been very, very poor, and though I'm in good financial shape these days I still have the necessary skills and could go through another round of serious poverty without any great stress. Every so often, just to make sure I haven't lost it, I pare my expenses to the bone and live poor for a week or two; it's a useful exercise, and it also frees up money for other uses.

replies.title: (no subject)

The usual way to mess up a geomantic reading is to overthink it, and that's what you've done here. What's the rule? If you ask a yes or no question in a shield chart and the majority of figures in the court (judge + witnesses) is negative, the answer is "no." replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, to begin with, as a polytheist, I don't have to disbelieve in anybody's deities! That's why I accepted consecration as a Gnostic bishop in 2004, from the same John Gilbert who brought me into the Druid order I headed for 12 years. That's also why I accepted an invitation to become a Martinist.

The order with which I'm affiliated is descended directly from Papus' Ordre Martiniste, which as you may know drew most of its inspiration and its core initiation from Louis-Claude de St.-Martin. Papus himself specified that anyone who believed in a Supreme Being could join, so long as they were comfortable with the Christian symbolism used in the order. Like most Martinist orders these days, the one I've joined can also pass on Pasqually's initiations, but I won't be receiving those -- I have no personal call to spiritual warfare, which is the essence of Pasqually's work. I do hope to qualify for some of the other initiations that are part of the broader Martinist tradition, such as the Rose+Croix d'Orient, which focuses on healing and blessing -- much more my style!

comments.title: Paedrig's Shrines and places of offering
comments.content:
So I recently had the chance to explore my land after many long years of exile. I set up a circle of stones to create an area for doing my daily ritual work. However, the circle kept getting literally washed away by rainwater, so I took it as a sign I needed to find another place.

I was searching for somewhere with level ground, somewhere that seemed... right. And then ai cam upon it. A small square of bricks, laid out according to the cardinal directions. I had placed them there years ago, with the intent of creating a fire pit. And now? Now I immediately knew the ground right next to it should be levelled and smoothed and made into a new ritual circle, set up so I would face this square construction which I immediately thought of as a shrine.

I know what diety to make this shrine to - Jupiter. I made a pentacle long ago, a solpmonkc talisman to jupiter, and it has become a centerpiece of my rotual circle, set just below an obelisk in the center through which I channel energy flows up and down through my central channel. It felt right - I felt like I've been building my relationahip with Jupiter for some time, and have been showered with blessings and wealth, including the very land I stood upon.

I called on Jupiter and dedicated the shrine to him, and then performed my basic moe daily ritual - sphere of protection, breath meditation, healing hands - but it seems like thats only a beginning. Like I should do more. I feel inspired to make a burnt offering to all the gods - its something I used to do annually and havemt for some time, and I did promise them an offering if I got my home back - but I feel like I need to go beyond for Jupiter.

Any guidance on dedicating a shrine to a god? To Jupiter specifically? This wont be the last shrine I dedicate as I am inspired to fill my woods with them, but it is the first and I want to do this right. This is sort of me taking my relationship with this god to the next level, and any guidance you can offer would be greatly appreciated.

replies.title: Re: Paedrig's Shrines and places of offering
replies.content: To be utterly traditional, the obelisk at the center should be anointed with olive oil and then have a crown or wreath of sweet-smelling herbs placed atop it. Do that and you'll have done exactly what ancient Greek Pagans did back in the day to create a shrine to a god or goddess. Then burn some incense to Jupiter, recite the Orphic Hymn to him, and pour a libation of wine. You'll get a response.

comments.content: What are the best ways to work on letting go of attachments that are unhealthy to latch onto? Be they possessions, people, achievements, etc. I am not capable of most ritual magic due to karma, so I’d like to ask what else exists. The OSA lessons can be helpful, I know.

replies.title: Re: Letting go
replies.content: The OSA lessons or some similar practice are essential. Attachments happen for emotional reasons, usually linked to unresolved childhood experiences; working back to those, processing the emotions, and releasing them -- that's the one way to let go of unhealthy attachments. Awareness is the only solvent that will do the trick.

comments.title: Improving magical items
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

If I have once created a magical item, let’s say a protective amulet, is it considered a finished object that can never be improved, or is it fine to re-enchant it, especially as one’s skills improve? I have made a few things earlier in my progress that I feel I could do a better job at, and would prefer not to start over. Also, what about alterations to the physical substance of it?

I will admit to long ambivalence regarding such things, with deep roots in my Catholic past, i.e. fretted much about what to do with all the excess rosaries and sacramentals I’d acquired. Different problem but in some sense, the same at its root.

replies.title: Re: Improving magical items
replies.content:
It really depends. Magical working tools should be consecrated repeatedly -- it builds their power over time. General purpose talismans, ditto. Talismans made for some specific, time-limited purpose ought to be set aside once that purpose has been fulfilled, since it's not effective to put more than one specific intention into a given item. Physical alterations tend to be problematic, but here again, it depends on details.

The rosaries and sacramentals -- well, are you still comfortable using them in something like their original way? If so, use them. If not, since they've been consecrated to a specific religious intention, I recommend giving them freely to someone who will use them with that intention in mind.
